air-quality-and-odour-identification-and.metaDescription: Learn to identify, assess and deodorise indoor odours from mould, smoke, VOCs and biological sources. Free air quality course for restoration pros.
air-quality-and-odour-identification-and.imageAlt: Restoration technician using an air scrubber to deodorise a water-damaged Australian home interior

og.title: Air Quality & Odour: Identification & Deodorisation | CARSI
og.description: Free CARSI course on finding and treating indoor odours at the source — microbial, smoke, chemical and biological — with air scrubbers, hydroxyl and ozone generators, carbon and thermal fogging.

faq.q: What deodorisation methods does this course cover?
faq.a: It covers source removal, chemical neutralisation (enzyme cleaners, odour counteractants and masking agents), oxidation with ozone and hydroxyl generators, and physical methods including activated carbon filtration, air scrubbers and thermal fogging.

faq.q: Is masking a smell the same as deodorising?
faq.a: No. The course stresses that masking agents only hide odours temporarily and can irritate occupants. Effective deodorisation identifies and removes the source through cleaning, drying, ventilation and targeted treatment, then verifies the result.

faq.q: What is the difference between ozone and hydroxyl generators?
faq.a: Ozone generators produce a strong oxidant and must only be used in unoccupied, sealed spaces because of health risks. Hydroxyl generators produce hydroxyl radicals that can be used safely in occupied areas and leave no harmful residue.

faq.q: Who is this course for and does it cost anything?
faq.a: It is a free course for professionals in restoration, environmental cleaning, HVAC and property management who deal with odours from water, fire, mould, biohazards or pet and tobacco smells. There are no prerequisites.

courseJsonLd.name: Air Quality and Odour: Identification and Deodorisation Essentials
courseJsonLd.description: A CARSI course on identifying, assessing and deodorising indoor air odours from microbial, smoke, chemical and biological sources using proven source-removal and treatment methods.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — open entry

avian-influenza-awareness-restoration-ia.seoTitle: H5 Bird Flu Awareness for Restoration Teams | CARSI
avian-influenza-awareness-restoration-ia.metaDescription: A free, calm CARSI awareness course for Australian restoration, IAQ and facility teams: report sightings correctly, protect workers and document work.
avian-influenza-awareness-restoration-ia.imageAlt: Facility professional recording observations and following the reporting pathway calmly at an Australian coastal site.

og.title: H5 Bird Flu Awareness for Restoration & Facility Teams | CARSI
og.description: A calm, evidence-led CARSI awareness course for Australian restoration, IAQ and facility teams: communicate clearly, follow the reporting pathway, protect workers, stay in scope and document work.

faq.q: What should I do if I find a sick or dead bird?
faq.a: Do not touch it. Avoid the area and keep children and pets away. Record the location and what you see, take photos or video only if safe, and report to the Emergency Animal Disease Hotline on 1800 675 888.

faq.q: Is this veterinary or diagnostic training?
faq.a: No. It is awareness training only — not veterinary, medical, wildlife-handling or government biosecurity response training. The role it teaches is to report, protect workers, document and communicate within scope, not to diagnose or certify a site as disease-free.

faq.q: What can restoration and IAQ professionals do within scope?
faq.a: They can keep site notes and photo records, manage access control and exclusion zones, plan worker safety and PPE records, keep cleaning and SDS records, document client communication in RestoreAssist.app, and escalate to the correct authority pathway.

faq.q: Can I describe a dry-fogging or disinfection method as required for H5?
faq.a: No. Disinfection claims must not exceed the product label, SDS, regulator position or evidence base. Methods such as hydrogen-peroxide dry fogging must not be described as required by Australian Government H5 control measures unless a written authority direction and product evidence support that claim.

courseJsonLd.name: Australian H5 Bird Flu Awareness for Restoration, IAQ and Facility Professionals
courseJsonLd.description: A calm, evidence-led CARSI awareness course for Australian restoration, IAQ and facility professionals on communicating correctly, following the official reporting pathway, protecting workers and documenting field work during current Australian H5 bird flu detections. Awareness training only — not veterinary, medical, wildlife-handling or government biosecurity response training.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — awareness training for restoration, IAQ, facility, strata and insurance-support professionals.

commercial-floor-care-schools-childcare.seoTitle: Commercial Floor Care for Schools & Childcare | CARSI
commercial-floor-care-schools-childcare.metaDescription: A free CARSI course in contract-ready floor care for schools and childcare: carpet, tile and grout, vinyl strip and seal, and pressure washing.
commercial-floor-care-schools-childcare.imageAlt: Cleaning contractor stripping and sealing a vinyl floor with wet-floor barriers in an empty Australian school hall.

og.title: Commercial Floor Care for Schools & Childcare — Free CARSI Course
og.description: A free CARSI course taking cleaners to a contract-ready floor-care service for schools and childcare — carpet, tile and grout, vinyl strip and seal, pressure washing, plus the compliance it expects.

faq.q: Does this course award IICRC CECs?
faq.a: No. This is professional development and contract-readiness training. It is not an IICRC-accredited course and does not award IICRC Continuing Education Credits (CECs). Where it references a standard such as ANSI/IICRC S100, it treats it as a recognised standard of care, not law.

faq.q: What floor-care services does the course cover?
faq.a: The four jobs that win school and childcare work: carpet cleaning (hot water extraction and low-moisture encapsulation), tile and grout cleaning, vinyl strip and seal, and pressure washing with run-off control.

faq.q: How is slip resistance verified after sealing?
faq.a: On the basis of AS 4663, which measures slip resistance of existing, in-service surfaces (as distinct from AS 4586, which classifies new surfaces). AS 4663 measures and classifies only — it does not set cure or re-entry times, which come from the product SDS/TDS.

faq.q: How should pressure-washing run-off be managed?
faq.a: Keep it out of stormwater, which generally flows untreated to waterways. Confine the area, collect the wash water and direct it to sewer — which needs prior written approval, a trade-waste agreement, from the water authority.

courseJsonLd.name: Commercial Floor Care for Schools & Childcare Contracts
courseJsonLd.description: A free CARSI professional-development course taking experienced cleaners to a contract-ready floor-care service for Australian schools and childcare centres, covering carpet, tile and grout, vinyl strip and seal, and pressure washing, each paired with the WHS, SDS, child-safety, slip-resistance and run-off requirements a contract expects. This is contract-readiness training; it is not an IICRC-accredited course and does not award IICRC Continuing Education Credits (CECs).
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: Hands-on cleaning experience recommended — suited to commercial cleaners and small cleaning-business owners.

documenting-and-reporting-air-quality-im.metaDescription: Monitor, document and report job-site air quality in cleaning and restoration — contaminants, monitoring tools, PPE and compliant reporting. AU course.
documenting-and-reporting-air-quality-im.imageAlt: Restoration worker logging particle counter readings on an Australian job site beside a HEPA air scrubber

og.title: Documenting & Reporting Air Quality Improvements | CARSI
og.description: A CARSI course on monitoring job-site air quality in cleaning and restoration — spotting contaminants like mould, dust, VOCs and gases, choosing the right monitoring tools and documenting findings.

faq.q: What air quality contaminants does this course cover?
faq.a: It covers the main job-site contaminants in cleaning and restoration: mould spores, dust and particulate matter, VOCs from cleaning products and solvents, asbestos in older buildings, and combustion gases such as carbon monoxide and nitrogen dioxide.

faq.q: Which tools are used to monitor air quality on site?
faq.a: The course explains particulate counters for dust, airborne mould testing kits, photoionisation detectors (PIDs) for VOCs, gas detection meters for toxic gases, and temperature and humidity sensors that flag conditions encouraging mould growth.

faq.q: Why is documenting air quality important?
faq.a: Documentation supports health and safety compliance, provides data for insurance claims and serves as evidence in disputes. The course shows how to record readings at regular intervals and note the actions taken to mitigate contaminants.

faq.q: How much does this course cost?
faq.a: The course is priced at AUD $28.98 and is open to cleaning and restoration professionals with no prerequisites.

courseJsonLd.name: Documenting and Reporting Air Quality Improvements
courseJsonLd.description: A CARSI course on monitoring, documenting and reporting job-site air quality in cleaning and restoration, covering common contaminants, monitoring tools, safety protocols and compliant reporting.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — open entry

dust-and-particulates-in-indoor-air-cont.seoTitle: Dust & Particulate Control in Indoor Air | CARSI
dust-and-particulates-in-indoor-air-cont.metaDescription: Control indoor dust and fine particles (PM10, PM2.5, ultrafine) with MERV and HEPA filtration, cleaning and source control. Free AU air quality course.
dust-and-particulates-in-indoor-air-cont.imageAlt: Technician HEPA-vacuuming carpet while reading a laser particle counter in an Australian office to control dust

og.title: Dust & Particulates in Indoor Air: Control & Cleaning | CARSI
og.description: A free CARSI course on managing indoor airborne dust — from PM10 and PM2.5 to ultrafine particles — through measurement, MERV and HEPA filtration, cleaning, HVAC upkeep and source control.

faq.q: What is the difference between PM10, PM2.5 and ultrafine particles?
faq.a: PM10 are coarse particles 10 micrometres or smaller, such as dust, pollen and mould spores. PM2.5 are fine particles 2.5 micrometres or smaller, often from combustion. Ultrafine particles are below 0.1 micrometres and can reach deep into the lungs and bloodstream.

faq.q: How do MERV and HEPA filters differ?
faq.a: MERV rates a filter's ability to trap particles, from MERV 8 (lint and dust mites) up to MERV 13 (bacteria and smoke). HEPA filters exceed the MERV scale for very fine particles. Higher-rated filters restrict airflow, so the system should be checked before upgrading.

faq.q: What cleaning methods reduce dust without stirring it up?
faq.a: The course recommends damp microfibre cloths over feather dusters, HEPA-equipped vacuums with sealed filtration, mopping after vacuuming, and avoiding dry sweeping — all of which limit the resuspension of settled dust back into the air.

faq.q: Is this course suitable for facility managers?
faq.a: Yes. It is a free course for restoration professionals, facility managers, environmental technicians and anyone responsible for indoor air quality, with no prerequisites.

courseJsonLd.name: Dust and Particulates in Indoor Air: Control and Cleaning Strategies
courseJsonLd.description: A CARSI course on identifying, measuring and controlling indoor airborne dust and particulates using filtration, cleaning, HVAC maintenance and source-control strategies.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — open entry

floor-care-onboarding-operational-readin.seoTitle: Professional Floor Care Onboarding & Readiness | CARSI
floor-care-onboarding-operational-readin.metaDescription: CARSI's internal floor-care onboarding: WHS, PPE and SDS, surface identification, cleaning and sealing methods, machinery, hazard control and job evidence.
floor-care-onboarding-operational-readin.imageAlt: Floor-care technician operating a rotary scrubbing machine with wet-floor signage in an Australian school corridor.

og.title: Professional Floor Care Onboarding — CARSI Operational Readiness
og.description: CARSI Maintenance Company's internal floor-care induction — health and hygiene, WHS and chemical safety, surface identification, methods, machinery, site setup, conduct, and a final assessment.

faq.q: Who is this onboarding for?
faq.a: It is the internal induction for CARSI Maintenance Company floor-care technicians, supervisors and facilities staff who deliver cleaning, polishing, sealing, maintenance and hygiene services across schools, childcare, education, public buildings and high-traffic commercial sites.

faq.q: Does this course award IICRC CECs?
faq.a: No. It is an internal operational-readiness induction and does not award continuing education credits. It supports good judgement and does not replace the SDS/TDS, standards, or a site's own rules.

faq.q: What does the final assessment involve?
faq.a: A supervised, end-to-end standard job assessed against the checklists: vehicle and equipment readiness, site arrival and setup, surface identification and correct method, safe machine operation and hazard control, professional conduct and confidentiality, and close-out with evidence and handover. A pass requires at least 85% overall and 100% on every safety-critical and reputation-critical item.

faq.q: What safety topics are covered?
faq.a: WHS duties, the hierarchy of controls, PPE selection from the SDS, reading Safety Data Sheets, chemical labelling and storage, spill response, and electrical and trip safety, including RCD/safety-switch use and test-and-tag referencing AS/NZS 3760.

courseJsonLd.name: CARSI Maintenance Company Onboarding — Professional Floor Care & Operational Readiness
courseJsonLd.description: CARSI Maintenance Company's internal onboarding for floor-care technicians and supervisors, covering health and hygiene, WHS and chemical safety, surface identification, cleaning and sealing methods, machinery, site setup, conduct and a final practical assessment. This is an internal induction and does not award continuing education credits (CECs).
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — internal induction for CARSI Maintenance Company floor-care staff.

hvac-systems-and-indoor-air-quality-what.seoTitle: HVAC & Indoor Air Quality for Technicians | CARSI
hvac-systems-and-indoor-air-quality-what.metaDescription: Improve indoor air quality through HVAC — airflow, filtration, ventilation, moisture and mould, ductwork and odour control. Free course for technicians.
hvac-systems-and-indoor-air-quality-what.imageAlt: HVAC technician inspecting evaporator coils and a filter in an Australian building's air handling unit for air quality

og.title: HVAC Systems & Indoor Air Quality: For Technicians | CARSI
og.description: A free CARSI course showing technicians how HVAC systems shape indoor air quality — airflow, MERV and HEPA filtration, UV-C and carbon, ventilation, moisture and mould, ductwork and odour control.

faq.q: How do HVAC systems affect indoor air quality?
faq.a: HVAC systems condition and circulate air throughout a building, so they can either remove or spread contaminants. Poorly maintained units distribute dust, mould spores, VOCs and odours, while well-maintained systems filter and dilute pollutants and control humidity.

faq.q: What filter should a technician choose?
faq.a: The course explains the MERV scale (1–16) and HEPA filters, which capture very fine particles. Higher-rated filters clean more but restrict airflow, so the system must be compatible. UV-C lamps and activated carbon filters can supplement mechanical filtration.

faq.q: How does HVAC relate to moisture and mould?
faq.a: Cooling coils condense and drain moisture from the air, but dirty coils, clogged drain lines, overflowing condensate pans or oversized systems can leave excess humidity. Relative humidity above 60 per cent encourages mould, dust mites and material breakdown.

faq.q: Who is this course for?
faq.a: It is a free course for HVAC technicians and service professionals who want to assess, maintain and improve indoor air quality through HVAC performance. There are no prerequisites.

courseJsonLd.name: HVAC Systems and Indoor Air Quality: What Every Technician Should Know
courseJsonLd.description: A CARSI course equipping technicians to assess, maintain and improve indoor air quality through HVAC performance — covering airflow, filtration, ventilation, moisture control, ductwork and odour management.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — open entry

introduction-to-air-quality-fundamentals.seoTitle: Indoor Air Quality Fundamentals Course | CARSI
introduction-to-air-quality-fundamentals.metaDescription: Foundational indoor air quality (IAQ) course for cleaning and restoration pros — contaminants, ventilation, monitoring tools and control strategies.
introduction-to-air-quality-fundamentals.imageAlt: Cleaning professional setting up a HEPA negative air machine and containment sheeting in an Australian home

og.title: Introduction to Air Quality Fundamentals | CARSI
og.description: A CARSI foundations course in indoor air quality for cleaning and restoration — pollutant types, ventilation and air changes per hour, monitoring tools, and strategies to keep indoor air safe.

faq.q: What are the main indoor air contaminants covered?
faq.a: The course groups indoor air pollutants into biological contaminants (mould, bacteria, viruses), chemical contaminants such as VOCs from cleaning agents and paints, and particulate matter like dust, soot and debris disturbed during cleaning or demolition.

faq.q: What is 'air changes per hour' and why does it matter?
faq.a: Air changes per hour (ACH) measures how many times the air in a room is replaced or cleaned each hour. Restoration work aims for a higher ACH to reduce contaminant load and support drying. Recommended rates vary by space type.

faq.q: What Australian standards or humidity ranges does the course reference?
faq.a: It references Australian Standard AS 1668.2 for mechanical ventilation design and recommends keeping indoor relative humidity between 30 and 60 per cent to limit mould and dust mites, alongside MERV 13 or higher filters for better fine-particle capture.

faq.q: Is this course suitable for beginners?
faq.a: Yes. It is a foundational course for cleaning and restoration professionals who are just starting out or reinforcing the basics, priced at AUD $29 with no prerequisites.

courseJsonLd.name: Introduction to Air Quality Fundamentals
courseJsonLd.description: A CARSI foundations course introducing indoor air quality for cleaning and restoration professionals — pollutant types, ventilation, monitoring tools and practical strategies to manage and improve indoor environments.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — open entry

introduction-to-creating-a-clean-air-env.seoTitle: Final Clearance & Air Quality Handover Course | CARSI
introduction-to-creating-a-clean-air-env.metaDescription: Learn to assess indoor air quality, run final clearance testing and hand over remediation projects safely. Australian-produced professional course.
introduction-to-creating-a-clean-air-env.imageAlt: Restoration technician using a particle counter to check indoor air quality during final clearance, Australian site.

og.title: Final Clearance & Air Quality: Remediation Handover | CARSI
og.description: A CARSI professional course on achieving a clean-air environment: air quality testing, visual inspections, clearance equipment and clear client handover after restoration or mould remediation.

faq.q: What is final clearance in air quality remediation?
faq.a: Final clearance is the stage where a restoration professional confirms all contamination has been removed and a space is safe to reoccupy. It verifies that hazardous airborne particles such as mould spores, dust and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) have been eliminated before the space is handed back to occupants.

faq.q: What air quality tests does the course cover?
faq.a: The course covers testing for airborne particulate matter (including PM2.5), airborne mould spores, volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and indicator gases such as carbon dioxide and carbon monoxide, using tools like particle counters and gas detectors, with results interpreted by a qualified indoor environmental professional.

faq.q: What equipment is used during final clearance?
faq.a: The course introduces air scrubbers, HEPA vacuums, moisture meters, hygrometers, airborne particle counters, gas detectors and personal protective equipment (PPE) used to test, clean and verify air quality before handover, including keeping relative humidity below 60% to prevent mould regrowth.

faq.q: What should a handover report include?
faq.a: A comprehensive handover report should summarise the initial assessment and scope of work, the remediation methods used, the final clearance inspection and test results, confirmation that agreed clearance criteria were met, photographic documentation, and any warranties plus preventative advice for the client.

courseJsonLd.name: Introduction to Creating a Clean Air Environment: Best Practices for Final Clearance and Handover
courseJsonLd.description: An introductory CARSI course on creating a clean-air environment, covering indoor air quality assessment, final clearance testing, visual inspection, documentation and professional client handover after restoration or remediation work.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — an introductory course for restoration and remediation professionals.

introduction-to-drying-educational-and-i.seoTitle: Drying Educational & Institutional Sites Course | CARSI
introduction-to-drying-educational-and-i.metaDescription: Free CARSI course on drying water-damaged schools, universities and institutions — protect laboratories, auditoriums, archives and campus infrastructure.
introduction-to-drying-educational-and-i.imageAlt: Restoration technician positioning drying equipment in a flood-affected university laboratory in Australia.

og.title: Drying Educational & Institutional Sites | Free CARSI Course
og.description: A free CARSI course on managing structural drying in schools, universities and institutions — safeguarding laboratories, auditoriums, data corridors, archives and campus communities during recovery.

faq.q: What types of sites does this course cover?
faq.a: The course covers drying and water damage recovery across educational and institutional sites, including university laboratories, auditoriums with expansive hardwood stages, underground IT and fibre-optic corridors, libraries, and archives holding irreplaceable artefacts and collections.

faq.q: How does the course address protecting sensitive laboratory equipment?
faq.a: It explains safeguarding laboratory exhaust ventilation, chemical trench drains and emergency eye wash stations from moisture, assessing damage to sensitive equipment such as scales, microscopes and reagents, and maintaining continuous monitoring to prevent contamination and disruption.

faq.q: What is incremental drying?
faq.a: Incremental drying divides affected wings into compartmentalised drying zones that are renovated on a staggered basis. This lets some areas reopen while others are still being repaired, avoiding lengthy full-facility shutdowns and making better use of resources.

faq.q: How can institutions maintain continuity during drying works?
faq.a: The course covers temporary relocations such as leasing modular classroom trailers, arranging alternative study spaces and support for affected students, and supplementary utility provisions like chilled-water bypasses from adjoining plants to keep critical research running.

courseJsonLd.name: Introduction to Drying Educational and Institutional Sites
courseJsonLd.description: A free introductory CARSI course on structural drying and water damage recovery in educational and institutional settings, covering special design features, shared infrastructure, building population factors, regulatory oversight and loss limitation tactics.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — an introductory course for restoration and facilities professionals.

introduction-to-drying-health-care-facil.seoTitle: Drying Health Care Facilities Course | CARSI
introduction-to-drying-health-care-facil.metaDescription: Free CARSI course on drying hospitals and healthcare facilities — infection control, containment, medical gas and protecting critical systems.
introduction-to-drying-health-care-facil.imageAlt: Restoration technician installing HEPA negative-air containment beside an active hospital ward in Australia.

og.title: Drying Health Care Facilities | Free CARSI Course
og.description: A free CARSI course on drying and water damage recovery in hospitals and healthcare facilities — filtration, barrier controls, medical gas systems, infection control and clearance verification.

faq.q: What makes drying healthcare facilities different from other buildings?
faq.a: Healthcare facilities require elevated filtration, strict containment barriers, and protected environments for immunocompromised patients, while safeguarding unique systems such as medical gas infrastructure, pharmacy clean rooms and biohazard waste flows — all without interrupting critical patient operations.

faq.q: What MERV filtration do hospitals use?
faq.a: MERV ratings run from 1 to 16, with higher numbers capturing finer particles. The course notes residential HVAC systems typically use MERV 6 to 8 filters, while hospitals commonly use MERV 13 or higher, often supplemented by HEPA negative-air machines during works.

faq.q: How are immunocompromised patients protected during drying works?
faq.a: The course covers secondary enclosures with additional airlocks, staged pressure differentials, UV scrubbers and HEPA discharge filtration, plus clearly demarcated containment zones and locked anterooms that keep construction dust and mould spores away from vulnerable patient wings.

faq.q: What is clearance verification in a hospital setting?
faq.a: Clearance verification is rigorous environmental testing before containment enclosures are removed — checking for lead dust particulates, VOC off-gassing and microbiological threats, typically via an independent third-party hygienist, with documented results provided to the relevant authorities before reoccupancy.

courseJsonLd.name: Introduction to Drying Health Care Facilities
courseJsonLd.description: A free introductory CARSI course on drying and water damage recovery in healthcare facilities, covering special design considerations, unique building systems, infection control risks, maintaining critical operations and remediation standards.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — an introductory course for restoration and healthcare facilities professionals.

introduction-to-drying-hospitality-and-l.seoTitle: Drying Hospitality & Lodging Sites Course | CARSI
introduction-to-drying-hospitality-and-l.metaDescription: Free CARSI course on water damage restoration in hotels and lodging — phased closures, content salvage, guest experience and safe reopening.
introduction-to-drying-hospitality-and-l.imageAlt: Restoration technician drying a flood-affected hotel guest room with air movers in an Australian lodging venue.

og.title: Drying Hospitality & Lodging Sites | Free CARSI Course
og.description: A free CARSI course on water damage restoration in hotels and lodging venues — limiting revenue loss, salvaging room contents, phased closures, heritage-accurate rebuilds and safe reopening.

faq.q: What does this course cover for hospitality water damage?
faq.a: The course covers managing water damage in hotels and lodging venues — reducing revenue and booking losses, salvaging room contents and porous textiles, staging structural repairs by wing and floor, heritage-accurate reconstruction, and post-remediation cleanup before reopening.

faq.q: How are guests and bookings managed during restoration?
faq.a: Reservations from unaffected areas are relocated to authorised alternative properties using enterprise booking databases, while phased reopening schedules are guided by rehabilitation timelines and confirmed event bookings to limit revenue interruption.

faq.q: How does the course approach heritage or period properties?
faq.a: It covers sourcing matching finishes such as quarried dimensional stone and period ceramic tiling, reprinting original deco wallpaper from archived samples, and engaging heritage architectural consultants to replicate period millwork and mouldings in line with a property's landmark status.

faq.q: What safety checks are needed before reopening?
faq.a: Before containment barriers are removed, the course calls for multivariate sensor testing of lead particulate dust, airborne mould spore counts and chemical VOC off-gassing against indoor air quality thresholds, verified by a licensed environmental inspector, plus ongoing post-remediation humidity monitoring.

courseJsonLd.name: Introduction to Drying Hospitality and Lodging Sites
courseJsonLd.description: A free introductory CARSI course on water damage restoration for hospitality and lodging sites, covering business operations impacts, room and content salvage, structural repair staging, reconstructive constraints and remediation cleanup.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — an introductory course for restoration and hospitality professionals.

introduction-to-drying-industrial-and-ma.seoTitle: Drying Industrial & Manufacturing Sites Course | CARSI
introduction-to-drying-industrial-and-ma.metaDescription: Free CARSI course on drying flood-affected industrial and manufacturing sites — containment, air-quality monitoring, material drying and compliance.
introduction-to-drying-industrial-and-ma.imageAlt: Restoration technician using air movers to dry a flood-affected Australian manufacturing plant floor.

og.title: Drying Industrial & Manufacturing Sites — Free CARSI Course
og.description: A free introduction to drying and restoring flood-affected industrial and manufacturing sites: containment, VOC air-quality monitoring, drying complex materials and securing regulatory clearance.

faq.q: What does the Introduction to Drying Industrial and Manufacturing Sites course cover?
faq.a: It is a free CARSI course on drying and restoring flood-affected industrial and manufacturing sites — protecting inventory and equipment, containing contaminants, monitoring air quality for volatile organic compounds (VOCs), drying complex materials, and meeting regulatory compliance requirements.

faq.q: How are complex materials dried after flooding at a manufacturing site?
faq.a: The course covers material-specific methods: desiccant drying pouches to draw subsurface humidity from dense metals such as aluminium moulds, replacing waterlogged porous insulations to prevent microbial growth, infrared thermal mapping to dry textured plastics evenly, and negative-pressure cycling to remove moisture from composite materials.

faq.q: How is a site cleared to resume operations after water damage?
faq.a: The course explains securing clearance certification through multistage quantitative indoor air-quality testing, comparing results against pre-disruption baseline reports to confirm contaminant concentrations have been reduced and the site meets environmental compliance before permits are reinstated.

faq.q: Who is this course for?
faq.a: It suits Australian restoration professionals, facility managers and emergency-response teams handling water-damage recovery at industrial or manufacturing premises. It is an introductory online course with no prior experience required.

courseJsonLd.name: Introduction to Drying Industrial and Manufacturing Sites
courseJsonLd.description: A free introductory CARSI course on drying and restoring flood-affected industrial and manufacturing sites, covering contaminant containment, air-quality monitoring, drying of complex materials, and securing regulatory clearance.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — introductory course, no prior experience required.

introduction-to-drying-transportation-an.metaDescription: Free CARSI course on drying flood-affected transport assets — containment, air-quality monitoring, drying metals, plastics and composites, and compliance.
introduction-to-drying-transportation-an.imageAlt: Restoration technician drying a flood-damaged vehicle interior with air movers in an Australian workshop setting.

og.title: Drying Transportation & Vehicles — Free CARSI Course
og.description: A free introduction to drying flood-affected transport assets: containing contaminants, monitoring air quality, and drying moisture-sensitive metals, plastics, insulations and composites.

faq.q: What does the Introduction to Drying Transportation and Vehicles course cover?
faq.a: This free CARSI course introduces how to dry and restore flood-affected assets — protecting equipment and stock, containing contaminants, monitoring indoor air quality, drying moisture-sensitive materials, and meeting regulatory compliance requirements.

faq.q: How are moisture-sensitive materials dried after flooding?
faq.a: The course covers material-specific drying: desiccant pouches to draw subsurface humidity from dense metals such as aluminium, replacing waterlogged porous insulations to prevent microbial growth, infrared thermal mapping to dry textured plastics evenly, and negative-pressure cycling to remove moisture from composite materials.

faq.q: Why is air-quality monitoring important during flood recovery?
faq.a: Monitoring detects volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and other airborne contaminants early, giving time to adjust ventilation and prevent contamination spreading. The course also covers multistage indoor air-quality testing to confirm a site is safe before operations resume.

faq.q: Is any prior experience needed?
faq.a: No. This is a free introductory online course with no prerequisites — suited to Australian restoration professionals and anyone starting out in flood-damage recovery.

courseJsonLd.name: Introduction to Drying Transportation and Vehicles
courseJsonLd.description: A free introductory CARSI course on drying and restoring flood-affected assets, covering contaminant containment, indoor air-quality monitoring, drying of moisture-sensitive materials, and regulatory clearance.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — introductory course, no prior experience required.

introduction-to-iaq-and-mould-understand.seoTitle: Mould & IAQ: Airborne Spread & Containment | CARSI
introduction-to-iaq-and-mould-understand.metaDescription: CARSI course on how mould spreads through the air and how to contain it — negative pressure, HEPA filtration, PPE and post-remediation air testing.
introduction-to-iaq-and-mould-understand.imageAlt: Mould remediation technician in PPE installing a negative-pressure containment barrier in an Australian home.

og.title: IAQ & Mould: Understanding Airborne Spread & Containment
og.description: Learn how airborne mould spores spread through buildings and HVAC systems, and the containment and remediation methods that protect indoor air quality — negative pressure, HEPA filtration and PPE.

faq.q: How does mould spread through the air in a building?
faq.a: Mould releases microscopic spores when a colony is disturbed by activity, airflow or physical contact. These spores stay suspended in the air and can travel through a building — especially via HVAC systems, on people's clothing and shoes, and through open doors and windows. High humidity, generally above 60%, accelerates growth and spore release.

faq.q: What containment methods stop mould spores spreading during remediation?
faq.a: The course covers creating negative pressure with air scrubbers or negative-air machines so air flows inward, sealing the work area with plastic sheeting attached to floor, walls and ceiling, isolating HVAC vents and ducts, using HEPA-filtered air scrubbers, and wearing PPE including HEPA respirators, suits, gloves and goggles.

faq.q: How is indoor air quality confirmed after mould remediation?
faq.a: Post-remediation air sampling measures airborne spore concentrations to confirm they are within safe limits. Ongoing steps include installing humidity sensors to keep relative humidity below 60%, regular inspections of moisture-prone areas and HVAC systems, and continued HEPA filtration.

faq.q: What are the health risks of airborne mould?
faq.a: Inhaled mould spores can cause reactions ranging from sneezing and coughing to asthma attacks and skin rashes, with greater effects on sensitive people or those with respiratory conditions. Some moulds such as Stachybotrys chartarum (black mould) produce mycotoxins that can affect the immune system and contribute to respiratory illness with prolonged exposure.

courseJsonLd.name: Introduction to IAQ and Mould: Understanding Airborne Spread and Containment
courseJsonLd.description: A CARSI course on how airborne mould spores spread through buildings and HVAC systems, and the containment, remediation and monitoring methods that protect indoor air quality during mould remediation.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — introductory course, no prior experience required.

introduction-to-improving-indoor-air-qua.seoTitle: Indoor Air Quality After Water Damage Course | CARSI
introduction-to-improving-indoor-air-qua.metaDescription: CARSI course on improving indoor air quality after water damage — identifying mould, bacteria and VOCs, assessment tools, remediation and reporting.
introduction-to-improving-indoor-air-qua.imageAlt: Restoration professional using a moisture meter to assess air quality in a water-damaged Australian home.

og.title: Improving Indoor Air Quality After Water Damage — CARSI
og.description: Water damage introduces mould, bacteria, VOCs and allergens indoors. Learn to identify these contaminants, assess air quality with moisture meters, PIDs and air sampling, then remediate and report.

faq.q: What contaminants affect indoor air quality after water damage?
faq.a: Water damage commonly introduces mould, bacteria, volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and allergens such as dust mites. Mould can begin growing within 24 to 48 hours, floodwater can carry bacteria like Salmonella, E. coli and Legionella, and VOCs are released from wet paints, adhesives, sealants and flooring.

faq.q: What tools are used to assess air quality after water damage?
faq.a: The course covers moisture meters and infrared cameras to find hidden moisture, air sampling (spore traps, impaction devices and bioaerosol pumps) for airborne mould and particles, Photoionisation Detectors (PIDs) to measure VOCs, ATP testers to indicate biological contamination, and temperature and humidity sensors to keep relative humidity below 60%.

faq.q: How is indoor air quality improved after water damage?
faq.a: Remediation combines removing mould-affected materials under negative air pressure, dehumidification to control moisture, HEPA air scrubbers to capture fine particles and spores, activated carbon filters to absorb VOCs, and ventilation with air movers to dilute and remove airborne contaminants.

faq.q: Why is documentation important in water damage restoration?
faq.a: Documenting before-and-after readings — moisture levels, air sampling results, VOC concentrations and humidity — creates a record that the work met safety requirements. Clear reports are provided to the client, insurer and relevant authorities, and follow-up inspections confirm air quality stays at safe levels.

courseJsonLd.name: Introduction to Improving Indoor Air Quality After Water Damage
courseJsonLd.description: A CARSI course on restoring indoor air quality after water damage — identifying mould, bacteria, VOCs and allergens, assessing air quality with professional tools, and applying remediation and documentation best practice.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — introductory course, no prior experience required.

introduction-to-infrared-thermography-fo.metaDescription: Free introductory course on infrared thermography for moisture detection and drying in restoration: heat transfer, camera selection, safety and analysis.
introduction-to-infrared-thermography-fo.imageAlt: Australian restoration technician using an infrared thermal camera to detect moisture in a water-damaged wall

og.title: Infrared Thermography for Drying — Free CARSI Course
og.description: Learn how infrared thermography detects hidden moisture and guides drying decisions in restoration—covering heat transfer, camera technology, safety and result analysis. Free, Australian-produced.

faq.q: What is infrared thermography used for in drying?
faq.a: It helps identify surface temperature differences that indicate where moisture remains in materials, guiding the placement of drying equipment and helping verify drying progress across a restoration project.

faq.q: Does an infrared camera measure moisture directly?
faq.a: No. An infrared camera detects surface temperature patterns, not moisture itself. Cooler areas can suggest evaporative cooling, so readings should be confirmed with moisture meters, hygrometers or other complementary tools.

faq.q: What are the limitations of thermal imaging for moisture?
faq.a: Contact barriers such as vinyl wallpaper and dense materials can mask readings, and the camera generally cannot see interstitial layers between components, so physical access or complementary methods may be needed.

faq.q: Do I need experience to take this course?
faq.a: No. It is an introductory course covering thermal energy concepts, infrared camera technologies, moisture detection applications, safety protocols and result analysis from the basics.

courseJsonLd.name: Introduction to Infrared Thermography for Drying
courseJsonLd.description: An introductory course on applying infrared thermography to detect moisture and guide drying in water damage restoration, covering heat transfer principles, camera technology, safety protocols and result analysis.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — introductory level; no prior thermography experience required.

introduction-to-recovery-of-submerged-it.seoTitle: Recovery of Submerged Items and Contents | CARSI
introduction-to-recovery-of-submerged-it.metaDescription: Free course on recovering water-damaged and submerged contents: restorability, recovery logistics, preservation, cleaning and functionality checks.
introduction-to-recovery-of-submerged-it.imageAlt: Australian contents restoration specialist assessing and packing water-damaged salvaged items for recovery

og.title: Recovery of Submerged Items and Contents | CARSI
og.description: A free introductory course on restoring water-damaged contents: evaluating restorability, recovery logistics, preservation techniques, cleaning methods and validating functionality.

faq.q: How do you decide if a water-damaged item can be restored?
faq.a: Restorability depends on the item's material composition, how long it was submerged, the contamination exposure from the floodwater, and any pre-existing damage recorded against a documented baseline.

faq.q: Why does contamination affect contents recovery?
faq.a: Floodwater can carry contaminants that drying alone will not address, so water samples may be analysed to determine appropriate neutralisation chemistry and any special handling or PPE required to protect people and assets.

faq.q: What is vacuum freeze-drying used for?
faq.a: It suits delicate contents such as soaked paper and parchment, using freeze sublimation to remove moisture where direct heating would risk distortion or leave residual moisture that causes later deterioration.

faq.q: Why keep chain-of-custody records during recovery?
faq.a: Recording who handles each item and when supports clear accountability and provides evidence if questions later arise about missing items or how the collection was handled through the recovery process.

courseJsonLd.name: Introduction to Recovery of Submerged Items and Contents
courseJsonLd.description: An introductory course on evaluating and restoring water-damaged and submerged contents, covering restorability assessment, recovery logistics, initial preservation, cleaning methods and functionality validation.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — introductory level; no prior contents restoration experience required.

introduction-to-restoration-of-antiques-.seoTitle: Restoration of Antiques and Fine Furnishings | CARSI
introduction-to-restoration-of-antiques-.metaDescription: Free course on restoring water-affected antiques and fine furniture: material risks, safe handling, gentle cleaning, restorative methods and authenticity.
introduction-to-restoration-of-antiques-.imageAlt: Australian furniture conservator carefully cleaning a water-damaged antique timber cabinet in a workshop

og.title: Restoration of Antiques and Fine Furnishings | CARSI
og.description: A free introductory course on restoring antique and delicate furniture affected by moisture: bonding and fungal risks, safe handling, gentle cleaning, restorative techniques and protecting provenance.

faq.q: How does moisture damage antique furniture?
faq.a: Prolonged moisture can erode adhesives in aged glued joints, cause warping through dimensional change, encourage fungal colonisation in concealed corners, and trigger chemical degradation of finishes, metals and dyes.

faq.q: Why avoid harsh solvents on antiques?
faq.a: Concentrated solvents such as acetone can damage shellac and original finishes; gentler options like terpene microemulsions and plant-based poultices help lift soiling while preserving patina and authenticity.

faq.q: Why document provenance during restoration?
faq.a: Records such as invoices, shipping inventories, maker markings and photographic timelines substantiate ownership and authenticity, helping protect market value and defend against title or valuation disputes.

faq.q: Do I need restoration experience for this course?
faq.a: No. It is an introductory course covering material integrity risks, handling precautions, cleaning technique selection, restorative methodologies, and authentication and appraisal fundamentals.

courseJsonLd.name: Introduction to Restoration of Antiques and Fine Furnishings
courseJsonLd.description: An introductory course on restoring antique and delicate furniture affected by moisture, covering material integrity risks, safe handling, cleaning techniques, restorative methods, and authentication and appraisal.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — introductory level; no prior furniture restoration experience required.

introduction-to-ultraviolet-light-and-fl.metaDescription: Free course on ultraviolet light and fluorescence for water loss inspections: UVA, UVB and UVC properties, UV safety, equipment and moisture detection.
introduction-to-ultraviolet-light-and-fl.imageAlt: Australian restoration inspector using a UV light to reveal fluorescence and moisture in a darkened water-damaged room

og.title: Ultraviolet Light and Fluorescence | CARSI
og.description: A free introductory course on ultraviolet light and fluorescence for water loss inspections: UVA, UVB and UVC properties, UV safety, equipment selection, and moisture and microbial detection.

faq.q: How is UV light used in water loss inspections?
faq.a: Ultraviolet light can trigger fluorescence in moisture, dye tracers and residues, helping trace leak sources, map porous saturation, verify drying progress and flag possible microbial growth sites.

faq.q: What is the difference between UVA, UVB and UVC?
faq.a: UVA (315–400 nm) is lower energy and needs fewer precautions, UVB (280–315 nm) produces more visible fluorescence but requires more shielding, and UVC (below 280 nm) is germicidal and demands strict safety protocols.

faq.q: Is UV light safe to use during inspections?
faq.a: UV exposure carries skin and eye risks, so protective eyewear, appropriate clothing and routine exposure monitoring are used, with greater care needed for shorter UVB and UVC wavelengths.

faq.q: Can UV replace other moisture detection methods?
faq.a: No. UV works best alongside complementary tools such as infrared thermography and moisture mapping, which help confirm findings and locate hidden moisture not visible under UV alone.

courseJsonLd.name: Introduction to Ultraviolet Light and Fluorescence
courseJsonLd.description: An introductory course on ultraviolet light and fluorescence for water loss inspections, covering UVA, UVB and UVC properties, UV safety, equipment, moisture and microbial detection, and complementary technologies.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — introductory level; no prior UV inspection experience required.

introduction-to-water-damage-litigation-.seoTitle: Water Damage Litigation Support Course | CARSI
introduction-to-water-damage-litigation-.metaDescription: Free CARSI course in water damage litigation support: forensic moisture investigation, psychrometric evidence, scope analysis, reporting and testimony.
introduction-to-water-damage-litigation-.imageAlt: Restoration professional reviewing psychrometric drying logs and moisture readings at an Australian water-damage site.

og.title: Water Damage Litigation Support — Free CARSI Course
og.description: A free CARSI course on forensic water-damage investigation — moisture source tracing, psychrometric evidence, scope analysis, report writing and preparing for expert testimony.

faq.q: What does water damage litigation support involve?
faq.a: It is the forensic side of restoration: tracing moisture sources, analysing material failure, assessing code compliance, gathering psychrometric and sensor data, benchmarking scope and cost, and preparing evidence and reports that stand up in legal proceedings.

faq.q: Do I need prior experience to take this course?
faq.a: No. It is an introduction that aims to give a basic but comprehensive understanding of forensic investigations, so it suits restoration professionals who are new to litigation-support work. There are no prerequisites.

faq.q: What evidence and documentation does the course cover?
faq.a: It covers psychrometric records, sensor-mapping history, intrusive and laboratory sampling, demonstrative diagrams, catalogued technical literature, financial audit trails, evidence chain of custody, and expert-testimony scripting.

faq.q: How much does the course cost?
faq.a: It is free. The course is published at no cost and can be taken online in Australian English.

courseJsonLd.name: Introduction to Water Damage Litigation Support
courseJsonLd.description: An introductory CARSI course in forensic water-damage investigation and litigation support, covering moisture source tracing, evidence gathering, scope analysis, report creation and expert-testimony preparation.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — introductory course for restoration professionals.

moisture-mould-and-indoor-air-quality-un.seoTitle: Moisture, Mould & Indoor Air Quality | CARSI
moisture-mould-and-indoor-air-quality-un.metaDescription: Free CARSI course on how moisture drives mould growth and harms indoor air quality — detection, health effects, drying, remediation and documentation.
moisture-mould-and-indoor-air-quality-un.imageAlt: Environmental technician using a moisture meter and thermal imaging to inspect a damp wall in an Australian building.

og.title: Moisture, Mould & Indoor Air Quality — Free CARSI Course
og.description: A free CARSI course on the link between moisture, mould and indoor air quality — spore activation, MVOCs, high-risk materials, HVAC and humidity, health effects, air sampling, drying and remediation.

faq.q: How quickly can mould grow after water damage?
faq.a: Once moisture is introduced, dormant spores can germinate and grow into mature colonies within 24 to 48 hours, especially in warm, nutrient-rich conditions. This is why rapid drying is central to preventing mould.

faq.q: What indoor humidity level encourages mould?
faq.a: Relative humidity consistently above 60% strongly favours mould growth. Keeping indoor RH between 30% and 50% is widely recommended to limit mould and maintain occupant comfort.

faq.q: Does visible mould always require air sampling?
faq.a: No. In many cases visible mould is enough to warrant remediation. Air sampling is most useful for suspected hidden mould, real estate transactions or legal cases, and results should be interpreted by a qualified professional against outdoor comparison samples.

faq.q: Who is this course for?
faq.a: Restoration, building, environmental health and facilities professionals who want a stronger foundation in identifying, investigating and managing moisture and mould, and in protecting indoor air quality.

courseJsonLd.name: Moisture, Mould, and Indoor Air Quality: Understanding the Link
courseJsonLd.description: A free CARSI course explaining how moisture activates mould and affects indoor air quality, covering detection tools, health effects, HVAC and humidity control, drying, safe remediation and documentation.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — suitable for restoration, building, environmental health and facilities professionals.

using-air-scrubbers-and-afds-to-improve-.seoTitle: Air Scrubbers & AFDs for Job Site Air Quality | CARSI
using-air-scrubbers-and-afds-to-improve-.metaDescription: Free CARSI course on using air scrubbers and AFDs on the job site: filters, HEPA, negative pressure, ACH calculations, maintenance and compliance.
using-air-scrubbers-and-afds-to-improve-.imageAlt: Restoration technician setting up a HEPA air scrubber for negative pressure containment on an Australian job site.

og.title: Air Scrubbers & AFDs — Job-Site Air Quality | CARSI
og.description: A free CARSI course on air filtration devices — how air scrubbers work, choosing HEPA, carbon and pre-filters, recirculation vs negative pressure, calculating Air Changes per Hour and maintenance.

faq.q: What is the difference between recirculation and negative pressure mode?
faq.a: In recirculation mode the AFD filters air and returns it to the same room to reduce particulate levels. Negative pressure mode vents filtered air outside to create containment, drawing air into the work area so contaminants cannot escape — used for mould, asbestos or infection control.

faq.q: How do you calculate Air Changes per Hour (ACH)?
faq.a: ACH = (CFM × 60) ÷ room volume in cubic feet. For example, a 500 CFM scrubber in a 10,000 cubic foot room gives 3 ACH. The course notes typical targets of 2–4 ACH for general work, 4–6 for mould remediation and 6–12 for asbestos or infection control.

faq.q: What do HEPA filters capture?
faq.a: HEPA filters capture 99.97% of airborne particles 0.3 microns or larger, including mould spores, bacteria and fine dust. A pre-filter catches larger debris first, and an activated carbon filter can be added for odours and VOCs.

faq.q: Does an air scrubber remove the need for PPE?
faq.a: No. An AFD is one layer of protection, not a replacement for PPE. Appropriate respirators, gloves, goggles and coveralls should still be worn, especially during demolition or work involving hazardous materials.

courseJsonLd.name: Using Air Scrubbers and AFDs to Improve Job Site Air Quality
courseJsonLd.description: A free CARSI course on operating air filtration devices (air scrubbers) to control airborne contaminants on job sites, covering filters, recirculation and negative pressure, ACH calculations, maintenance, safety and documentation.
courseJsonLd.coursePrerequisites: None — suitable for restoration professionals, contractors and environmental technicians.
